skill.name: moodle-external-api-development
skill.description: Create custom external web service APIs for Moodle LMS, including parameter schemas, service registration, permission checks, database access, error handling, and testing workflows.
skill.summary: Build secure Moodle external APIs with validation, services, permissions, and testing guidance.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, weak-crypto, and reconnaissance patterns, but review shows these are mostly Markdown examples, Moodle terms, placeholders, and documentation links in SKILL.md. No prompt injection, malicious intent, credential exfiltration, or executable payload was found. The main real concern is copyable debugging guidance that could create world-writable log directories and expose SQL or stack traces in production.

medium_findings.title: Insecure Debug Logging Guidance
medium_findings.description: The guide shows a Moodle API logging helper that creates a plugin log directory with mode 0777 and records user identifiers, exception messages, last SQL, and stack traces. This is not executable malware, but copied production code could expose sensitive Moodle data through overly broad filesystem permissions or verbose logs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines directly show permissive directory creation and detailed error logging. The risk is content guidance risk, not confirmed malicious execution, because the file is a Markdown skill guide.

low_findings.title: Static Command And Network Alerts Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The external command and network alerts are triggered by fenced PHP, Bash, and JavaScript snippets, including curl examples against placeholder Moodle URLs and Moodle AMD require usage. These examples support the stated API development purpose and do not run when the skill is loaded.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious patterns occur inside Markdown code fences or resource links. There is no script file, installer, or instruction that causes these commands to execute automatically.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto And Reconnaissance Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography findings map to ordinary words and Moodle field names, such as Moodle, methodname, description, and grade_grades. The reconnaissance findings map to SQL examples and Moodle table references used to explain parameterized database access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line review shows benign documentation text and table names rather than cryptographic code or host reconnaissance. No hashing function, network scanner, or system inventory behavior is present.

dangerous_patterns.title: Permissive Log Directory Creation In Example Code
dangerous_patterns.description: The example uses mkdir with 0777 permissions for a Moodle data directory. Users should restrict permissions and follow Moodle file handling practices before using similar code in production.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The permission mode is explicitly shown in the example. The risk depends on users copying the guidance into deployable code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Verbose SQL And Stack Trace Logging In Example Code
dangerous_patterns.description: The example logs last SQL and full stack traces after exceptions. This can disclose schema details, user identifiers, or sensitive operational context if logs are exposed.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines directly log detailed SQL and stack trace data. The concern is mitigated because this is illustrative Markdown rather than live code.

content.value_statement: Moodle plugin teams need external APIs that validate data, enforce permissions, and return stable structures. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through Moodle web service patterns, examples, and testing steps.
actual_capabilities: Explains the Moodle execute_parameters, execute, and execute_returns pattern.
Shows how to register external functions in db/services.php.
Covers validate_parameters, validate_context, capabilities, and parameterized SQL.
Provides examples for read APIs, write APIs, quiz operations, groups, and availability rules.
Includes testing paths for the Moodle test client, curl, and Moodle JavaScript AJAX.
Lists common Moodle tables and plugin file structure for external API work.
limitations: It is a guidance skill and does not scaffold files automatically.
Examples use placeholder plugin names, URLs, users, and table names.
It does not replace Moodle version-specific API documentation.
Debug logging examples need hardening before production use.

use_cases.title: Create A Plugin Web Service
use_cases.description: Build a custom Moodle external function with validated parameters, return structures, and service registration.

use_cases.title: Review API Security
use_cases.description: Check a Moodle endpoint for context validation, capability checks, safe SQL, and appropriate write operations.

use_cases.title: Test Integration Access
use_cases.description: Prepare test calls for Moodle REST, AJAX, and web service client workflows using realistic parameters.

prompt_templates.title: Plan A Simple Read API
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner planning for a Moodle external function.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design a Moodle external API that reads course data. Include parameters, permissions, return fields, and service registration steps.

prompt_templates.title: Implement Service Registration
prompt_templates.prompt: Guide me through registering a Moodle external function in db/services.php for a local plugin. Explain function naming, type, AJAX support, and capabilities.

prompt_templates.title: Harden Existing Endpoint
prompt_templates.scenario: Security review of a Moodle API.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Moodle external API design for validate_parameters, validate_context, require_capability, safe database access, and production-safe logging.

prompt_templates.title: Design Complex Quiz Workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced API for quiz and group operations.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Moodle external API that creates quiz activities from question categories, applies user-specific access restrictions, and keeps write operations transaction-safe.

output_examples.input: Ask for a new Moodle read API for quiz attempts.
output: A proposed function name and plugin namespace.
Required userid and courseid parameters with Moodle validation types.
Capability and context checks before database access.
A return structure that reports attempt counts clearly.

output_examples.input: Ask for a security review of an external API plan.
output: A checklist covering parameter validation, context validation, role capabilities, SQL placeholders, write operation safety, and log exposure.
Specific remediation notes for missing checks or unsafe debugging practices.

output_examples.input: Ask how to test a Moodle web service endpoint.
output_examples.output: The response outlines Moodle web service setup, token creation, test client usage, REST call parameters, and AJAX testing considerations.

best_practices: Validate parameters before business logic and validate context before accessing course data.
Use Moodle capabilities and parameterized database methods for every external function.
Limit production logging to necessary details and protect log storage permissions.
anti_patterns: Do not concatenate user input into SQL strings.
Do not expose write APIs without explicit capability checks.
Do not copy debug logging examples into production without redaction and permission hardening.

faq.question: What type of Moodle work does this skill support?
faq.answer: It supports custom external web service APIs for Moodle plugins, including read APIs, write APIs, quiz workflows, and integrations.

faq.question: Does it generate a complete Moodle plugin?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples, but developers must create and test the plugin files.

faq.question: Does it cover Moodle permission checks?
faq.answer: Yes. It emphasizes validate_context and require_capability before accessing or changing Moodle data.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude Code and Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill declares support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Are the curl examples ready for production?
faq.answer: No. They use placeholder URLs, users, passwords, and tokens that must be replaced with secure test values.

faq.question: What should I check before publishing an API built from this guidance?
faq.answer: Check parameter validation, context validation, capabilities, SQL placeholders, transaction handling, service exposure, and log redaction.
